content:
# Genesis Chapter 36 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20v–21v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 33–35 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0037

---

**1** Now these are the generations of Esau, that is, Edom.

**2** Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan: Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon the Hivite,

**3** and Basemath the daughter of Ishmael, sister of Nebaioth.

**4** And Adah bore to Esau: Eliphaz. And Basemath bore Reuel.

**5** And Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These are the sons of Esau who were born to him in the land of Canaan.

**6** Then Esau took his wives, his sons, his daughters, and all the members of his household, his livestock, all his beasts, and all his possessions that he had acquired in the land of Canaan, and he went to a land away from his brother Jacob.

**7** For their possessions were too great for them to dwell together, and the land of their sojournings could not support them because of their livestock.

**8** So Esau settled in the hill country of Seir. Esau is Edom.

**9** Now these are the generations of Esau, the father of Edom, in the hill country of Seir.

**10** These are the names of Esau's sons: Eliphaz the son of Adah the wife of Esau, Reuel the son of Basemath the wife of Esau.

**11** The sons of Eliphaz were: Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz.

**12** Now Timna was a concubine of Eliphaz, Esau's son, and she bore to Eliphaz: Amalek. These are the sons of Adah, Esau's wife.

**13** These are the sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These were the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife.

**14** These were the sons of Oholibamah the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon, Esau's wife: she bore to Esau — Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.

**15** These are the chiefs of the sons of Esau. The sons of Eliphaz the firstborn of Esau: chief Teman, chief Omar, chief Zepho, chief Kenaz,

**16** chief Korah, chief Gatam, chief Amalek. These are the chiefs of Eliphaz in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Adah.

**17** These are the sons of Reuel, Esau's son: chief Nahath, chief Zerah, chief Shammah, chief Mizzah. These are the chiefs of Reuel in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife.

**18** These are the sons of Oholibamah, Esau's wife: chief Jeush, chief Jalam, chief Korah. These are the chiefs from Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, Esau's wife.

**19** These are the sons of Esau — that is, Edom — and these are their chiefs.

**20** These are the sons of Seir the Horite, who inhabited the land: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,

**21** Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, the sons of Seir, in the land of Edom.

**22** The sons of Lotan were: Hori and Hemam; and Lotan's sister was Timna.

**23** These are the sons of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.

**24** These are the sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah — he is the Anah who found the yemim in the wilderness, when he was tending the donkeys of Zibeon his father.

**25** These are the children of Anah: Dishon and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah.

**26** These are the sons of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.

**27** These are the sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.

**28** These are the sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.

**29** These are the chiefs of the Horites: chief Lotan, chief Shobal, chief Zibeon, chief Anah,

**30** chief Dishon, chief Ezer, chief Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, according to their chiefs in the land of Seir.

**31** Now these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel.

**32** Bela the son of Beor reigned in Edom, and the name of his city was Dinhabah.

**33** And Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned in his place.

**34** And Jobab died, and Husham from the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.

**35** And Husham died, and Hadad the son of Bedad, who struck Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Avith.

**36** And Hadad died, and Samlah from Masrekah reigned in his place.

**37** And Samlah died, and Shaul from Rehoboth by the River reigned in his place.

**38** And Shaul died, and Baal-hanan the son of Achbor reigned in his place.

**39** And Baal-hanan the son of Achbor died, and Hadar reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Pau, and his wife's name was Mehetabel the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-zahab.

**40** Now these are the names of the chiefs of Esau, according to their clans and their locations, by their names: chief Timna, chief Alvah, chief Jetheth,

**41** chief Oholibamah, chief Elah, chief Pinon,

**42** chief Kenaz, chief Teman, chief Mibzar,

**43** chief Magdiel, chief Iram. These are the chiefs of Edom — that is, Esau the father of Edom — according to their dwelling places in the land of their possession.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of six manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 36 spans Folios 20v–21v, across three manuscript images.

   **Folio 20v** (GEN_036_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 20. This is PDF page 39 of the West Semitic Research Project sequence. The page contains the conclusion of Genesis 35 in its upper portions — including the brief account of Isaac's death and burial by Esau and Jacob (35:27–29) — and then continues into the beginning of Genesis 36. The three-column layout with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is clearly legible throughout. The distinctive layout of the Leningrad Codex manuscript is fully visible: the masorah parva annotations appear in the inter-column margins, and at least one masorah magna annotation is visible in the lower margin. Genesis 36 begins with the standard toledot formula (אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו) marked by a pe-parashah division, and the folio carries the text through approximately verse 14 — covering the toledot introduction (v. 1), Esau's wives and sons (vv. 2–5), Esau's separation from Jacob and migration to Seir (vv. 6–8), the second toledot heading in the hill country of Seir (v. 9), and the extended genealogy through the sons and grandsons of Esau including the concubine Timna and Amalek (vv. 10–14). The text is legible throughout, with full pointing and cantillation visible.

   **Folio 21r** (GEN_036_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 21. This is PDF page 40. This page carries the central portions of Genesis 36: the listing of the allufim (chiefs) of the sons of Esau (vv. 15–19), the sons of Seir the Horite and their genealogy (vv. 20–28), the chiefs of the Horites (vv. 29–30), the kings-of-Edom list beginning with the editorial note about the Israelite monarchy (v. 31) and continuing through the successive royal deaths (vv. 32–39). The three-column layout is fully legible. The distinctive formula "and [NAME] died and [NEXT NAME] reigned in his place" repeats through the royal list; the cantillation marks are consistent and legible. The obscure word הַיֵּמִם (yemim) at verse 24 is clearly readable on this folio. The alluf lists and the Horite genealogy are legible in the right-hand columns.

   **Folio 21v** (GEN_036_page_003.jpg): The verso of Folio 21. This is PDF page 41. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 36 and the beginning of Genesis 37. Genesis 36 concludes with the final alluf list arranged by territorial divisions (vv. 40–43), ending with the formula "Esau the father of Edom" — a closing identification matching the opening of verse 1. The boundary between Genesis 36 and 37 is clearly visible on this folio; a parashah marker indicates the new unit beginning at 37:1 ("And Jacob settled in the land of his father's sojournings"). The text of Genesis 36's closing verses is legible with full vocalization.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Three Vaticanus pages were read directly.

   **Page 33** (GEN_036_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 33 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0037). The page heading at top center indicates this is the Genesis 35/36 boundary area. The continuous uncial Greek text in three columns is legible. The left columns contain the conclusion of Genesis 35 and the opening of Genesis 36 in the LXX. The LXX text begins Genesis 36 with Αὗται δὲ αἱ γενέσεις Ησαυ ("Now these are the generations of Esau"), consistent with the MT's toledot formula. The opening wife-list (vv. 1–5) and migration account (vv. 6–8) are present on this page. The LXX orthography of proper names shows characteristic Greek transliteration patterns: Ησαυ for עֵשָׂו, Ελιφας for אֱלִיפָז, etc.

   **Page 34** (GEN_036_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 34 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0038). The left-margin chapter notation "CAP XLII" is visible. This page carries the bulk of Genesis 36 in Greek, covering the allufim lists (vv. 15–19), the Horite genealogy (vv. 20–30), and the kings-of-Edom list (vv. 31–39). The critical verse 24 with the yemim is present; the LXX renders ἰαμιν (or the equivalent), attempting a transliteration of the obscure Hebrew. The kings list shows the LXX's reading of verse 39, where the LXX attests Αραδ (Harad/Arad) for the king MT calls Hadar/Hadad — an important textual variant documented below.

   **Page 35** (GEN_036_vaticanus_page_003.jpg): Vaticanus page 35 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0039). The header notation "CAP XLIII" is visible at the top. This page contains the final alluf list (vv. 40–43) and the transition into Genesis 37. The LXX's rendering of the territorial alluf designations is visible, with some name variants relative to the MT.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "These are the generations of Esau, that is, Edom" — אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם (v. 1)**

The chapter opens with the standard toledot formula — אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת (elleh toledot — "these are the generations/account of begettings of") — applied here to Esau. This is the ninth occurrence of the toledot formula in Genesis (2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12; 25:19; 36:1) and one of two in this chapter: a second toledot heading appears at verse 9 (אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם, "the generations of Esau, the father of Edom, in the hill country of Seir"), slightly modified to include "father of Edom" and the geographical specification. The double toledot heading (vv. 1 and 9) has attracted considerable critical discussion: some scholars see two originally independent sources combined; others read verse 1 as a superscription to the whole chapter and verse 9 as the opening of the hill-country genealogy proper. The formal translation preserves both headings as they stand in the MT.

The appositional identification הוּא אֱדוֹם (hu Edom — "he is Edom," or "that is, Edom") appears four times in this chapter (vv. 1, 8, 19, 43), functioning as an editorial anchor that insists on the identification throughout. This repetition is theologically significant: the chapter is not merely antiquarian genealogy but an act of naming — Edom is being definitively constituted as a people with Esau as their patriarchal founder. The formal translation renders הוּא as "that is" throughout, consistent with established English convention for the appositional demonstrative.

The word תֹּלְדוֹת (toledot — "generations") is rendered "generations" throughout, preserving the formal equivalent and maintaining consistency with all other toledot headings in the LOGOS translation of Genesis.

**2. Esau's Wives: The Name Discrepancy Problem (vv. 2–3)**

Genesis 36:2–3 names Esau's three wives as: (1) עָדָה בַּת אֵילוֹן הַחִתִּי (Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite); (2) אָהֳלִיבָמָה בַּת עֲנָה בַּת צִבְעוֹן הַחִוִּי (Oholibamah the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon the Hivite); and (3) בָּשְׂמַת בַּת יִשְׁמָעֵאל (Basemath the daughter of Ishmael).

These names conflict with those given in two earlier passages. Genesis 26:34 states: "Esau took as wives Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hittite and Basemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite." Genesis 28:9 states: "Esau went to Ishmael and took as his wife, besides the wives he had, Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael, Abraham's son, the sister of Nebaioth."

The discrepancies are systematic: In Genesis 26:34, the first wife is "Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite" (here she is "Adah daughter of Elon the Hittite"); the second in Genesis 26:34 is "Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite" (here she is Oholibamah, who carries no Hittite connection); in Genesis 28:9, the Ishmaelite wife is "Mahalath" (here she is "Basemath"). The name "Basemath" migrates: it belongs to an Elon-daughter in Genesis 26:34 but to the Ishmael-daughter in Genesis 36:3. The name "Adah" appears here but nowhere in the earlier passages.

These discrepancies have generated three main explanatory approaches: (1) multiple wives with multiple names (two-name individuals were common in the ancient world); (2) independent source documents with their own traditions that were combined without harmonization; (3) scribal confusion at some point in transmission history. The formal translation follows the MT of Genesis 36 without harmonization, rendering the names as they stand. The cross-references are noted here rather than inserted into the text. The LXX presents the same names as the MT for Genesis 36 and does not resolve the discrepancy.

A subsidiary question appears in verse 2: Oholibamah is identified as בַּת עֲנָה בַּת צִבְעוֹן (daughter of Anah, daughter of Zibeon). The word בַּת (bat) here must mean "daughter" for Oholibamah relative to Anah, but then also "daughter" for Anah relative to Zibeon — making Anah male (Zibeon's son), which is confirmed by verse 20 (where Anah appears among the sons of Seir the Horite). The second בַּת in verse 2 therefore functions as "granddaughter" in terms of family relationship, though the formal translation follows the Hebrew's own term "daughter" twice and allows the genealogical context (vv. 20–25) to clarify the relationship. Some manuscripts and the LXX read υἱοῦ (son) for the second usage, understanding it as Anah the son of Zibeon — a reading that simplifies the syntax.

**3. "Went to a land away from his brother Jacob" — אֶרֶץ מִפְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו (v. 6)**

Esau's departure from Canaan is described in terms that deliberately recall the separation of Abraham and Lot in Genesis 13:5–12. The language is nearly identical: "their possessions were too great" (כִּי הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב, v. 7; compare Genesis 13:6 — כִּי הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב), "the land could not support them," and the separation because of livestock. The echo is structural: just as Lot, the second figure in the Abraham-Lot pair, moved east and away (toward Sodom), so Esau, the second figure in the Jacob-Esau pair, moves away to Seir/Edom. The departure is narrated without divine drama or negative judgment — Esau simply leaves, and the land of Canaan remains for Jacob. The formal translation renders מִפְּנֵי (mippenei — literally "from the face of") as "away from" — the standard rendering of this prepositional phrase when indicating spatial separation from a person. "Because of" (some translations) is also defensible, but "away from" captures both the spatial and relational dimensions of the departure. The LXX renders ἀπὸ προσώπου Ιακωβ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ αὐτοῦ ("from the face of Jacob his brother"), consistent with the MT.

**4. "Esau settled in the hill country of Seir. Esau is Edom." — הַר שֵׂעִיר / עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם (v. 8)**

The verse is a two-clause summary: settlement established, identification confirmed. הַר שֵׂעִיר (har Se'ir — "the hill country of Seir" or "Mount Seir") is the highland region southeast of the Dead Sea, later known as Edom. The place-name שֵׂעִיר (Seir) is connected by folk etymology to שֵׂעָר (se'ar — "hair, hairiness") — and Esau is described at birth as "all his body like a hairy cloak" (כֻּלּוֹ כְּאַדֶּרֶת שֵׂעָר, Genesis 25:25), his name's connection to "red" (אֲדמוֹנִי) founding the folk etymology of "Edom" (red). The triple identification — Esau-Seir-Edom — is woven through the chapter at several points (vv. 1, 8, 9, 19, 43) as a repeated affirmation of ethnic and territorial coherence. The formal translation renders הוּא as "is" in the shorter appositional formula of verse 8 and as "that is" in the fuller constructions.

**5. "Concubine" — פִּילֶגֶשׁ (pilegesh) (v. 12)**

Timna is identified as פִּילֶגֶשׁ אֱלִיפַז (pilegesh Eliphaz — "concubine of Eliphaz"). The term פִּילֶגֶשׁ (pilegesh — "concubine, secondary wife") designates a woman of recognized but lower-status union — a secondary wife who bears legal children but does not have the full status or rights of the primary wife. The institution is attested throughout the patriarchal and pre-monarchic periods (Abraham's Hagar and Keturah; Jacob's Bilhah and Zilpah; Gideon's unnamed concubine; Samson's relation with the woman of Timnah). In this context, Timna's status as concubine is the means by which Amalek, the son she bears, is identified genealogically with Esau through Eliphaz, while simultaneously being set apart from the line of the primary wives. Amalek is in the lineage but not in the center of the lineage. This matters narratively: Amalek will be Israel's paradigmatic enemy (Exodus 17:8–16; Numbers 24:20; Deuteronomy 25:17–19; 1 Samuel 15). The formal translation renders "concubine" throughout for pilegesh. The LXX renders παλλακή (pallakē — "concubine"), consistent with the MT.

Note also that Timna appears again in verse 22 as Lotan's sister — a daughter of Seir the Horite. She thus belongs to the indigenous Horite population, and her union with Eliphaz (Esau's son) is an intermarriage between the incoming Edomite line and the indigenous Horite population of Seir. The name Timna also appears in verse 40 as one of the territorial alluf-designations, suggesting that her descendants became a recognized clan within Edom.

**6. The Allufim (Chiefs) — אַלּוּפֵי (allufei) (vv. 15–19, 29–30, 40–43)**

The term אַלּוּף (aluf, plural אַלּוּפִים allufim) is one of the most distinctive terms in Genesis 36, appearing eighteen times in this chapter and virtually nowhere else in the Pentateuch. The root is connected to אֶלֶף (elef — "thousand, clan-group") and אָלַף (alaf — "to learn, to be accustomed" or "to lead, to be chief"). The alluf is a clan-leader, the head of a tribal sub-unit, possibly holding both political and military authority. The term is peculiar to Edomite and Horite social structure in Genesis — the parallel lists for Israel use שֵׁבֶט (shevet — "tribe") and נָשִׂיא (nasi — "prince, leader"), not aluf. The formal translation renders אַלּוּף consistently as "chief," following the consensus of major English translations (ESV, NASB, JPS, NIV) and reflecting the political-clan leadership function of the term. "Duke" (KJV) is historically problematic as an anachronistic feudal term. "Leader" (some modern renderings) is too generic. "Chief" preserves both the political authority and the clan-lineage dimension. The LXX renders ἡγεμών (hēgemōn — "leader, commander, governor"), a term of political authority consistent with the MT's sense.

The alluf lists appear in three clusters: the sons of Esau as allufim (vv. 15–19), the sons of Seir/Horites as allufim (vv. 29–30), and the territorial allufim of Esau (vv. 40–43). The first two clusters organize by genealogical descent; the third cluster is organized "according to their clans and their locations" (לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לִמְקֹמֹתָם — vv. 40, 43) — by territory rather than family line. This shift from genealogical to territorial organization in the final alluf list mirrors a political development: the Edomite clans had become associated with fixed territorial dwelling-places, marking the transition from a mobile patriarchal social structure to a settled political geography.

**7. "The yemim" — הַיֵּמִם (v. 24)**

Verse 24 reads: וְזֶה עֲנָה אֲשֶׁר מָצָא אֶת הַיֵּמִם בַּמִּדְבָּר בִּרְעֹתוֹ אֶת הַחֲמֹרִים לְצִבְעוֹן אָבִיו ("he is the Anah who found the yemim in the wilderness, when he was tending the donkeys of Zibeon his father"). The word הַיֵּמִם (ha-yemim — "the yemim") is one of the great lexical puzzles of the Hebrew Bible. It appears nowhere else in the Bible in this form or apparent meaning. Ancient and medieval translators handled it variously:

- The Vulgate (Jerome): aquas calidas ("hot springs / hot waters") — understanding it as a place-name or natural feature.
- The Targum Onkelos: גִּיבָרַיָּא ("giants, mighty men") — understanding it as a type of person.
- The Peshitta: connects it to a root for "water."
- Saadiah Gaon: mules — understanding that Anah discovered the hybrid animal by crossbreeding donkeys with horses.
- Modern scholarship: most proposals center on either (a) an unattested word for "hot springs," (b) a proper noun for a place or people, (c) "mules" (following a cognate Arabic or Aramaic connection), or (d) the Giants/Nephilim type figures known from other ancient Near Eastern literature.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders τοὺς Ιαμιν (tous Iamin — "the Iamin/Yamim"), treating the word as a proper noun for a people-group or place. The formal translation retains the Hebrew term "yemim" in transliteration rather than choosing among the competing interpretations. This is consistent with the LOGOS practice of preserving genuinely obscure terms in transliteration (cf. the treatment of kivrat-ha'arets at Genesis 35:16) and is the most honest formal-equivalence solution: the text records that Anah found something remarkable in the wilderness — the term is preserved, and the note records the uncertainty. The word functions rhetorically to mark Anah as a notable discoverer (מָצָא, matza — "he found"), a mini-narrative embedded in the genealogy that elevates one figure above the others.

**8. "Before any king reigned over the children of Israel" — לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (v. 31)**

This phrase is the most discussed editorial notation in Genesis 36, and indeed one of the most significant in the entire Pentateuch. The verse reads: וְאֵלֶּה הַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ("Now these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel"). The phrase לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (lifnei meloch melech livnei Yisrael — "before a king reigned as king over the children of Israel") presupposes that there eventually will be (or already has been) a king over Israel — which requires knowledge of the Israelite monarchy, specifically the Saul-David period. From the perspective of narrative chronology, this is placed in the patriarchal period, centuries before any Israelite king. The narrator is looking forward from the patriarchal era to an Israelite monarchy that lies ahead — or, if the text is read from the perspective of the final composer/editor, the notation is an explanatory gloss: Edom had a king-list before Israel did.

The formal translation renders the phrase directly: "before any king reigned over the children of Israel." The formal equivalent of לִפְנֵי (lifnei — "before, in front of, prior to") is "before." The construction מְלָךְ מֶלֶךְ (meloch melech — infinitive absolute + finite verb: "a king reigned as king" or "any king reigned") is rendered "any king reigned" — the idiomatic English equivalent for the Hebrew emphasis. The phrase בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (benei Yisrael — "children of Israel / sons of Israel") is rendered "the children of Israel" as throughout the LOGOS translation. The LXX renders πρὸ τοῦ βασιλεῦσαι βασιλέα ἐν Ισραηλ (pro tou basileusai basilea en Israēl — "before a king reigned in Israel"), consistent with the MT.

**9. "Hadar" versus "Hadad" — הֲדַר / הֲדַד (v. 39)**

The final king in the Edomite king-list (v. 39) is given as הֲדַר (Hadar) in the MT of Genesis 36, while the parallel passage in 1 Chronicles 1:50–51 reads הֲדַד (Hadad). The Leningrad Codex is unambiguous: the final king is Hadar (with the letter resh, ר) at Genesis 36:39. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads Αραδ (Arad) — a different form again. The textual tradition is thus tripartite: MT-Genesis has Hadar; MT-Chronicles has Hadad; LXX has Arad/Araad. Additionally, unlike all seven kings before him, Hadar/Hadad is not said to have died (the formula "and X died" is absent). This omission is exegetically significant: it has been read as (a) an intentional marker that Hadar was still reigning when the list was composed; (b) an accidental scribal omission; (c) a deliberate structural device signaling the end of the succession-list genre. The parallel in 1 Chronicles 1:50–51 supplies a death notice for Hadad, suggesting the omission in Genesis 36 may be secondary. The formal translation follows the MT of Genesis 36 — rendering the name as "Hadar" — and the matter is documented here. The LXX variant is noted in the table below.

**10. The Final Alluf List: Territorial Organization (vv. 40–43)**

The final alluf list (vv. 40–43) differs from the earlier alluf lists in its organizing principle. Verse 40 introduces the list with the phrase לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לִמְקֹמֹתָם בִּשְׁמֹתָם (le-mishpechotam li-meqomotam bi-shemotam — "according to their clans, according to their locations, by their names"). This threefold organizing structure — clan, location, name — is absent from the earlier alluf lists, which organize purely by descent. The shift indicates that this final list represents a later stratum of Edomite social organization in which territorial identity has become as important as genealogical identity. Some of the names in this list (Timna, Oholibamah, Kenaz) appear earlier in the chapter as personal names, here reused as territorial/clan designations — a common phenomenon in ancient genealogical literature where personal names become eponyms for the territories their bearers founded or ruled.

The formal translation renders the list's header: "these are the names of the chiefs of Esau, according to their clans and their locations, by their names." The closing formula of the chapter — אֱדוֹם לְמֹשְׁבֹתָם בְּאֶרֶץ אֲחֻזָּתָם הוּא עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם (hu Esav avi Edom — "he is Esau the father of Edom") — closes the chapter with the same appositional double identification that opened it. The chapter is framed by Esau's identity: opening with "that is, Edom" (v. 1) and closing with "Esau the father of Edom" (v. 43).

**11. "Fathered" and "bore" — יָלַד / יָלְדָה**

Following established LOGOS convention, the verb יָלַד (yalad) with a male grammatical subject is rendered "fathered" (e.g., v. 4: "Adah bore to Esau — Eliphaz"; v. 5: "Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah"). With a female subject, the verb is rendered "bore" (v. 4: "Adah bore to Esau"). This distinction preserves the grammatical gender marking of the Hebrew and reflects the different physiological realities denoted. The formal translation is consistent throughout the chapter.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 36

Pages 33–35 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible throughout, though the manuscript's age and the density of the uncial hand make some readings uncertain. Key variants observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 36:1 | אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם ("these are the generations of Esau, that is, Edom") | Αὗται δὲ αἱ γενέσεις Ησαυ, αὐτὸς ἐστιν Εδωμ ("these are the generations of Esau; he is Edom") | MT followed | Both consistent; LXX uses αὐτός ἐστιν ("he is") for הוּא |
| 36:2 | הַחִוִּי ("the Hivite") — Zibeon's ethnic identity | ὁ Χορραῖος ("the Horite") | "the Hivite" (MT) | LXX reads Horite (Χορραῖος) for Hivite (Hivvi). The MT at 36:20 identifies Seir as "the Horite"; calling Zibeon Horite (LXX) makes him consistent with v. 20. MT's "Hivite" may reflect a different tradition or scribal confusion of similar letters (ו/ר, waw/resh). The formal translation follows MT. |
| 36:3 | אֲחוֹת נְבָיוֹת ("sister of Nebaioth") | ἀδελφὴ Ναβαιωθ ("sister of Nebaioth") | "sister of Nebaioth" | Consistent |
| 36:11 | צְפוֹ (Zepho) | Σωφαρ (Sophar) | "Zepho" (MT) | LXX renders a different form; MT followed; 1 Chron 1:36 has צֵפִי (Zephi), a further variant |
| 36:14 | בַּת עֲנָה בַּת צִבְעוֹן ("daughter of Anah daughter of Zibeon") | θυγατρὸς Ανα υἱοῦ Σεβεγων ("daughter of Anah son of Zibeon") | "daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon" (MT, literally) | LXX reads υἱοῦ ("son") for the second בַּת ("daughter"), making Anah explicitly the son of Zibeon (which is genealogically consistent with v. 20). MT's double "daughter" is syntactically unusual; LXX's reading may preserve a more accurate tradition or represent a clarifying translation. Formal translation follows MT's surface form. |
| 36:22 | הֵמָם (Hemam) | Αιμαν (Aiman) | "Hemam" (MT) | Minor transliteration variant; 1 Chron 1:39 has הוֹמָם (Homam) |
| 36:24 | הַיֵּמִם ("the yemim") | τοὺς Ιαμιν ("the Iamin/Yamim") | "the yemim" (transliterated) | LXX treats as a proper noun for a people or place; MT term is a hapax of uncertain meaning; formal translation preserves the Hebrew in transliteration per LOGOS practice for genuine obscurities |
| 36:26 | חֶמְדָּן (Hemdan) | Αμαδαν (Amadan) | "Hemdan" (MT) | Minor variant; 1 Chron 1:41 has חַמְרָן (Hamran), a further variant |
| 36:31 | לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ("before any king reigned over the children of Israel") | πρὸ τοῦ βασιλεῦσαι βασιλέα ἐν Ισραηλ ("before a king reigned in Israel") | "before any king reigned over the children of Israel" | MT and LXX consistent in meaning; LXX slightly shorter ("in Israel" vs. "over the children of Israel") |
| 36:35 | הֲדַד (Hadad) | Αδαρ (Adar) | "Hadad" (MT) | LXX renders Adar; MT attests Hadad for this king (v. 35, not the final king); see note on v. 39 below |
| 36:39 | הֲדַר (Hadar) | Αραδ (Arad) | "Hadar" (MT) | The most significant textual variant in the chapter. MT-Genesis reads הֲדַר (Hadar, with resh); 1 Chronicles 1:50 reads הֲדַד (Hadad, with dalet); LXX reads Αραδ (Arad — a completely different name, possibly reflecting a different tradition or a corruption of Hadad/Hadar). The formal translation follows the Leningrad Codex reading: Hadar. |
| 36:39 | No death formula for Hadar | LXX similarly lacks a death formula | "Hadar" (no death formula) | Both MT and LXX agree in omitting the standard "and X died" formula for the final king, unlike 1 Chronicles 1:51 which supplies a death notice for Hadad |
| 36:40 | עַלְוָה (Alvah) | Γωλα (Gola) | "Alvah" (MT) | LXX diverges significantly for several of the final alluf names; MT followed throughout |
| 36:43 | הוּא עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם ("he is Esau the father of Edom") | αὐτὸς Ησαυ πατὴρ Εδωμ ("he is Esau, father of Edom") | "Esau the father of Edom" | Consistent; LXX omits the article, slightly more condensed |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 36 — Edom Constituted**

Genesis 36 is the most systematically organized genealogical chapter in the book of Genesis. Its structure is not a single list but a series of overlapping and interlocking registers that together accomplish something more complex than mere name-preservation: they constitute Edom as a complete political and ethnic entity. The chapter can be analyzed in five distinct literary units:

**Unit I — Esau's Household in Canaan (vv. 1–5):** The toledot heading, followed immediately by the wife-list and the sons born in Canaan. This unit establishes Esau's legitimacy as a patriarch: he has wives, sons, and wealth in the land. The use of the standard toledot formula links Esau's genealogy to the same literary tradition that records Abraham's, Isaac's, and Jacob's lines.

**Unit II — The Separation and Settlement (vv. 6–8):** Esau leaves Canaan with his entire household and settles in Seir. This brief narrative unit is the pivotal transition: it establishes geographical and political separation between Jacob/Israel and Esau/Edom — not as expulsion, conflict, or divine judgment, but as an organic consequence of abundance. The land cannot hold both brothers. The echo of the Abraham-Lot separation (Genesis 13) dignifies Esau's departure as a patriarchal-era precedent. Esau moves away; Canaan remains Jacob's.

**Unit III — The Hill-Country Genealogy (vv. 9–19):** A second toledot heading reorients the genealogy geographically — "the generations of Esau, the father of Edom, in the hill country of Seir." The genealogy then expands outward: sons (vv. 10–11, 13), with the concubine line (v. 12, yielding Amalek), then conversion of the sons into alluf-registers (vv. 15–19). The structure moves from individuals to political offices — from begettings to governance.

**Unit IV — The Horite Genealogy (vv. 20–30):** The sons of Seir the Horite. This is a distinct genealogical tradition — the pre-Edomite indigenous population of the region — woven into the Esau genealogy to provide the full ethnic landscape of Edom. The Horites are not Esau's descendants but the people among whom Esau settled. Their inclusion here is ethnographically significant: Edom is not simply Esau's family but a complex formed from Esau's line and the indigenous Horite population. The intermarriage visible in the Timna notice (v. 22 — Timna is a daughter of Seir the Horite; v. 12 — Timna became Eliphaz's concubine) and in the Oholibamah notices (she is a daughter of the Horite Anah) makes the amalgamation concrete. Edom is a composite people.

**Unit V — The Kings List (vv. 31–39):** The roster of eight kings who reigned before the Israelite monarchy. This list is unique in the Pentateuch: no other passage in the Five Books of Moses presents a king-list. The Edomite king-list does not follow the genealogical pattern — the kings are not sons of sons in an unbroken line; succession is not hereditary (no king is the son of the preceding king). This matches the historical reality of many ancient Near Eastern polities where kingship rotated among tribal groups or was elective rather than dynastic. The list is organized by the death-succession formula — "X died and Y reigned in his place" — borrowed from the annalistic tradition of ancient historiography.

The list's most striking feature is its framing (v. 31): these kings reigned "before any king reigned over the children of Israel." This editorial marker is the only explicit anachronistic perspective in the Genesis narrative. It is a moment of extraordinary transparency: the narrator, standing at a point after the Israelite monarchy has been established, looks back at the patriarchal era and notes that Edom had kings before Israel did. Edom precedes Israel in political development. This is not presented as a competitive claim or a source of shame; it is simply noted. But it resonates with the larger Esau-Jacob dynamic: Esau consistently arrives first (firstborn, first settled, first to have kings) while the promise and the blessing ultimately rest with Jacob-Israel.

**Unit VI — The Territorial Allufim (vv. 40–43):** The final alluf list organized by territory rather than genealogy. This unit closes the chapter and closes the book of Esau's generations. It signals the completion of the patriarchal phase of Edom's existence and the beginning of its settled, territorial, politically organized existence. The chapter ends: "These are the chiefs of Edom — that is, Esau the father of Edom — according to their dwelling places in the land of their possession." The phrase "their possession" (אֲחֻזָּתָם, achuzatam) is the same term used for Abraham's burial ground at Machpelah (23:4, 9, 20 — "a burial possession") and for the land promises to Israel (17:8 — "an everlasting possession"). Edom has its possession; the promise to Israel of their possession remains outstanding in the Genesis narrative.

---

**The Amalek Notice (v. 12) — Enemy Origin Embedded in Genealogy**

The single verse that notes Timna's concubinage to Eliphaz and Amalek's birth is one of the most consequential notations in the entire Pentateuch for the subsequent narrative of Israel. Amalek will be Israel's first enemy in the wilderness (Exodus 17:8–16), identified by Moses as deserving eternal war ("the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation," Exodus 17:16). Amalek will be designated by Balaam as "the first of the nations" (Numbers 24:20) — a preeminent people, yet one whose "end is destruction." Amalek will be at the center of Saul's catastrophic failure and David's early victories (1 Samuel 15, 30). The book of Esther connects Haman the Agagite to the Amalekite royal line (Haman is an Agagite; Agag is Amalek's king in 1 Samuel 15). All of this flows genealogically from Genesis 36:12.

The formal genealogy places Amalek with care: he is the son of Eliphaz (Esau's firstborn by his primary wife Adah) — making Amalek a grandson of Esau — but born of a concubine, not a primary wife. He appears in the alluf-list of Eliphaz's sons (v. 16: "chief Amalek") — recognized as a clan-leader in his own right. Yet his origin through the secondary line (concubine) places him in a liminal position within the Edomite genealogy. The narrative of enmity between Amalek and Israel is thus grounded in this genealogy: Amalek is not an external enemy who appears from nowhere; he is Esau's grandson, part of the extended family whose relationship with Israel-Jacob has been one of competition, blessing-theft, and painful separation. The enmity is fraternal.

---

**Edom and Israel — The Typological Pair**

Genesis 36 is placed between two foundational Jacob narratives: the resolution of Jacob's story in chapter 35 (Bethel renewal, Rachel's death, Isaac's death) and the beginning of the Joseph narrative in chapter 37. Its placement here is not accidental. The toledot of Esau/Edom is given before the narrative moves to Joseph and the twelve tribes — it is a deliberate structural act: before Israel's story continues, Edom's story is completed. Edom receives its complete constitution as a people, with wives, sons, chiefs, Horite integration, kings, and territorial settlement — all completed and closed — before Israel's story has moved an inch beyond the patriarchal household.

This structural placement creates a deliberate theological contrast. Edom is complete. Israel is not yet. Edom has kings; Israel is still a single tent-dwelling family. Edom has a land of possession; Israel is still sojourning. Edom's genealogy is a closed archive; Israel's story is an open narrative. The formal genealogical completeness of Genesis 36 throws into relief the narrative incompleteness of the Jacob-Israel story: the covenant people are still waiting, still wandering, still in the middle of the promise's unfolding.

Yet the chapter also insists on continuity between Esau and Jacob. Esau and Jacob buried their father together (35:29). Esau moves away from Jacob not in anger (the anger of Genesis 27:41 has already been defused at their reunion in chapter 33) but because "their possessions were too great for them to dwell together" (v. 7) — the same fraternal abundance that separated Abraham and Lot, not fraternal enmity. The separation is economically motivated and narratively dignified. The relationship between Israel and Edom in Genesis 36 is finally one of completed and separated brotherhood — each in their own land, each constituted as a people, each carrying the blessing of patriarchal descent, but on different trajectories within the larger biblical story.

---
